article.title: Transcription factor StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites conferring late blight resistance in potato
article.license: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/), which permits unrestricted reuse, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 binds THT promoter and regulates THT expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p064.s006: EMSA results showed a shift in signal of biotinylated THT promoter that was inhibited in the presence of excess unlabelled competitor (Supplementary Fig. S6B). PMC4765800.p064.s007: These results indicate that StWRKY1 can specifically bind to the W1-box of the THT promoter and can regulate its gene expression.

events.event_label: CaWRKY27 overexpression induces jasmonic acid and ethylene signaling pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s003: Several studies have focused on model plants such as Arabidopsis and tobacco, but only a few focused on potato WRKYs. PMC4765800.p075.s004: In potato, StWRKY1 is strongly induced following a compatible interaction with P. infestans and treatment with potassium phosphite during infection inhibited pathogen spread. PMC4765800.p075.s005: Overexpression of VvWRKY1 in grapevines provided higher resistance to downy mildew Plasmopara viticola through higher expression of genes involved in the jasmonic acid pathway. PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar.

events.event_label: CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b confer resistance to Hemileia vastatrix
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar. PMC4765800.p075.s008: Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix. PMC4765800.p075.s009: These findings depict the involvement of the WRKY transcription factors in disease resistance, as described here in potato against P. infestans.

events.event_label: Late blight infection strongly induces sHSP17.8 expression in resistant potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s001: We detected significantly high fold changes of sHSP17.8 (fold change=40.35) in a resistant genotype of potato against late blight infection. PMC4765800.p078.s002: The presence of HSEs on the promoter region of the StWRKY1 gene in the resistant genotype, but not in the susceptible genotype (Supplementary Table S4), helped the sHSPs to interact and induce the transcription factor StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p078.s003: Small HSPs interact and provide stability to the proteins by acting as molecular chaperones. PMC4765800.p078.s004: LUC assay confirmed the transcriptional regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8.

events.event_label: sHSPs interact with and activate StWRKY1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s001: We detected significantly high fold changes of sHSP17.8 (fold change=40.35) in a resistant genotype of potato against late blight infection. PMC4765800.p078.s002: The presence of HSEs on the promoter region of the StWRKY1 gene in the resistant genotype, but not in the susceptible genotype (Supplementary Table S4), helped the sHSPs to interact and induce the transcription factor StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p078.s003: Small HSPs interact and provide stability to the proteins by acting as molecular chaperones. PMC4765800.p078.s004: LUC assay confirmed the transcriptional regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8.

events.event_label: Mds1 increases susceptibility to wheat gall midge and powdery mildew
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s009: Likewise, the induction of mitochondrial HSP22 occurred in tomato during oxidative stress and helped to provide adoptive responses. PMC4765800.p078.s010: Conflicting with these results, wheat sHSP, Mds1 present on chromosome 3AS increases susceptibility to wheat gall midge and powdery mildew fungus Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici.

events.event_label: RRI genes produce N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s001: To combat pathogen infection, plants have developed specific metabolic pathways to biosynthesize metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls as mechanical barriers. PMC4765800.p080.s002: Following pathogen inoculation, genes involved in the production of tyramine-derived HCAAs were 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, which were upregulated 2.13-, 2.98-, and 4.04-fold, respectively, in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 associates with 4-CL and THT
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes. PMC4765800.p080.s005: However, here, for the first time, StWRKY1 was associated with the promoters of downstream HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL and THT in vivo and in vitro. PMC4765800.p080.s006: The results of the EMSA and LUC assays confirmed that StWRKY1 physically interacts with and induces high-fold-change expression of downstream genes involved in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p080.s007: Likewise, the overexpression of VvWRKY1 induced a cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase and a caffeic acid O-methyl transferase gene in grape vines, providing higher resistance to downy mildew.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes contribute to secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s001: Based on integrated metabolomics and genomics data, the authors propose a model to explain the role of StWRKY1, together with HCAA biosynthetic genes, in mediating potato secondary cell wall thickening (Fig. 7). PMC4765800.p084.s002: Following P. infestans invasion, a hierarchy of genes is induced in the phenylpropanoid pathway starting from the induction of phospholipid metabolites (glycerophospholipids and sphingolipids), possibly by enzymes produced by the pathogen, activating MAPK (StMEK1), which in turn phosphorylates the transcription factor (StWRKY1). PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 transcriptionally activates RR genes contributing to HCAA biosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p062.s003: The constructs were transformed into potato protoplasts. PMC4765800.p062.s004: We found that StWRKY1 bound to the promoter region of RR genes, and drastically activated LUC reporter expression in 4-CL (45.55) and THT (47.00) promoters compared with vector alone (4.16) (Fig. 5B), suggesting the transcriptional regulation of HCAA biosynthesis.

events.event_label: Phospholipid metabolites activate MAPK cascade and oxidative bursts in plant defence signalling
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s001: We also looked into the signalling pathways which involved induction of the WRKY transcription factor and detected high fold changes in several induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p076.s002: These phospholipids act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways and activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression.

events.event_label: StMEK1 interacts with StWRKY1 to induce RR gene expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression. PMC4765800.p076.s005: In potato, StMEK1 was functionally characterised during defence reactions, which are also associated with StWRKY1, as was confirmed here by BiFC assay. PMC4765800.p076.s006: StMEK1 and StWRKY1 function together to induce the expression of downstream RR genes involved in plant defence.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s001: To combat pathogen infection, plants have developed specific metabolic pathways to biosynthesize metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls as mechanical barriers. PMC4765800.p080.s002: Following pathogen inoculation, genes involved in the production of tyramine-derived HCAAs were 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, which were upregulated 2.13-, 2.98-, and 4.04-fold, respectively, in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 silencing reduces HCAA metabolites
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s006: However, no significant reduction was observed in HCT transcripts in silenced plants (fold change=2.22) compared with resistant plants (2.41) and this was probably due to the absence of the W1-box in the HCT promoter (Fig. 6G and Supplementary Table S3). PMC4765800.p067.s007: We then tested the effect of StWRKY1 silencing on the production of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p067.s008: The RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine (fold change=10.13), N-feruloyloctopamine (6.65), feruloylputrescine (3.80), and feruloylagmatine (3.44) were reduced (P<0.05) after silencing (Table 2). PMC4765800.p067.s009: Taken together, these results provide compelling evidence that StWRKY1 is a positive regulator of late blight resistance through the reinforcement of secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: PtoWRKY60 overexpression upregulates PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5 and CPR5
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar. PMC4765800.p075.s008: Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix. PMC4765800.p075.s009: These findings depict the involvement of the WRKY transcription factors in disease resistance, as described here in potato against P. infestans.

events.event_label: N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylputrescine, feruloylagmatine and HCAAs participate in cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 regulates HCAA biosynthesis and secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p084.s005: Consequently, StWRKY1 protein interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL, TyDC, THT, and possibly others, inducing high-fold-change production of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, and others. PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: pathogen infection induces phospholipid metabolites that activate MAPK cascade and oxidative bursts
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 silencing increases disease severity, lesion length and pathogen biomass
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s001: To assess the effect of StWRKY1 in late blight resistance, the disease severity and biomass were quantified in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants. PMC4765800.p067.s002: The lesion length was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (26.19mm; AUDPC=80.68), significantly differing from the non-silenced plants (14.90mm; AUDPC=47.60). PMC4765800.p067.s003: Likewise, pathogen biomass was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (fold change=13.06) compared with non-silenced plants at 6 dpi (Fig. 6A, B). PMC4765800.p067.s004: Furthermore, the relative abundances of the transcripts of phenylpropanoid genes (4-CL, THT, TyDC, and HCT) were determined in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants with reference to the susceptible genotype (Fig. 6C–F).

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s001: We also looked into the signalling pathways which involved induction of the WRKY transcription factor and detected high fold changes in several induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p076.s002: These phospholipids act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways and activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. events.event_label: StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites contributing to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p001.s001: Transcription factor StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites conferring late blight resistance in potato

events.event_label: StWRKY1 mediates secondary cell wall thickening contributing to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p003.s001: StWRKY1 transcription factor mediates secondary cell wall thickening in potato by regulating expression of tyramine-related hydroxycinnamic acid amide biosynthetic genes, thus contributing to resistance to late blight disease.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 regulates HCAAs accumulation in secondary cell walls
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s009: Silencing of the StWRKY1 gene was associated with signs of reduced late blight resistance by significantly increasing the pathogen biomass and decreasing the abundance of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p004.s010: This study provides convincing evidence on the role of StWRKY1 in the regulation of downstream genes to biosynthesize HCAAs, which are deposited to reinforce secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: WRKY overexpression confers pathogen tolerance by activating phytohormone signaling pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p008.s001: WRKY domain-containing proteins are one of the largest families of transcriptional regulators in plants. PMC4765800.p008.s002: They have a DNA-binding domain called a W-box (TTGACC/T or WRKYGQK) and are involved in plant immune responses. PMC4765800.p008.s003: Overexpression of WRKY transcription factor increased tolerance to pathogen infection through induction of phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways in grapes and tobacco. PMC4765800.p008.s004: In contrast, the overexpression of WRKY reduced resistance mechanisms in rice and Capsicum plants.

events.event_label: Phenylpropanoid metabolites enhance cell wall thickening contributing to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin. PMC4765800.p009.s005: Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

events.event_label: Inoculation with P. infestans increases lesion length indicating late blight severity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p042.s001: Late blight severity in the potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody was assessed by measuring lesion length over time following inoculation with P. infestans clonal lineage US-8. PMC4765800.p042.s002: The initial symptoms were observed as brown-coloured lesions on the inoculated stems at 3 dpi in both F06025 and Shepody.

events.event_label: Pathogen inoculation increases fatty acids and alkaloids abundance in resistant genotype
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p050.s003: Thirty-four of the identified metabolites were phenylpropanoids, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylputrescine, and feruloylagmatine. PMC4765800.p050.s004: In addition to phenylpropanoids, 24 fatty acids and four alkaloids were induced in higher abundance in the resistant than in the susceptible genotype following pathogen inoculation.

events.event_label: Phospholipid metabolites activate MAPK cascade and oxidative bursts
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 interacts with HCAA biosynthetic gene promoters and contributes to transcriptional activity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p061.s001: StWRKY1 physically interacts with promoters of HCAA biosynthetic genes and exhibits transcriptional activity

events.event_label: Silencing reduces abundance of StWRKY1 and TyDC
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p082.s001: To further assess the effect of StWRKY1 on the resistance mechanisms, StWRKY1 was silenced in a resistant genotype, and silenced and non-silenced resistant plants were compared based on relative gene expression of HCAA biosynthetic genes, accumulation of their downstream biosynthesized RRI metabolites, amount of fungal biomass, and disease severity. PMC4765800.p082.s002: The transcript abundance of StWRKY1 and its regulating RRI genes, 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, was reduced (P<0.05) in silenced plants (fold change=1.16, 0.40, 0.44, and 0.71, respectively) compared with resistant plants (5.52, 2.13, 2.98, and 4.04, respectively). PMC4765800.p082.s003: However, no significant reduction was observed for HCT transcripts, probably due to the absence of the W1-box. PMC4765800.p082.s004: The abundance of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine was reduced 2-fold after silencing, resulting in a higher (P<0.001) biomass in silenced plants (fold change=13.06).

events.event_label: Silencing hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA:hydroxycinnamoyl transferase decreases dimethoxylated syringyl units and affects cell wall
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p082.s003: However, no significant reduction was observed for HCT transcripts, probably due to the absence of the W1-box. PMC4765800.p082.s004: The abundance of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine was reduced 2-fold after silencing, resulting in a higher (P<0.001) biomass in silenced plants (fold change=13.06). PMC4765800.p082.s005: This may be due to the metabolic profiling protocol, where metabolite extraction, detection, and identification were not comprehensive, and thus it is possible that other RR genes and associated metabolites are also involved in imparting resistance to late blight. PMC4765800.p082.s006: Correspondingly, silencing the hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA:hydroxycinnamoyl transferase affected cell wall strength by decreasing the dimethoxylated syringyl units of the lignin polymer in Nicotiana benthamiana stems. PMC4765800.p082.s007: Silencing of cellulose synthase genes affected cell wall formation in flax.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 induces HCAAs and contributes to secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s005: Consequently, StWRKY1 protein interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL, TyDC, THT, and possibly others, inducing high-fold-change production of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, and others. PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: RR genes encode enzymes 4-CL, HCT, THT, and TyDC involved in HCAA biosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s005: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2). PMC4765800.p051.s006: The expression of RR genes is transcriptionally regulated by the co-ordinated action of transcription factors which bind to cis-acting elements and regulate the expression of RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s007: Previous studies reported that StWRKY1 (GenBank accession number AJ278507.1; chromosome 5) is induced and co-regulated with class I endochitinase during the compatible interaction of potato and P. infestans.

PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: Pathogen infection increases abundance of multiple phospholipid metabolites
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression.

events.event_label: StMEK1 (MAPK) activates/phosphorylates StWRKY1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression. PMC4765800.p059.s005: To substantiate the interaction between StWRKY1 and StMEK1 in potato cells, a BiFC assay was performed in which YFPn fused to StWRKY1 (YFPn-StWRKY1) and YFPc fused to StMEK1 (StMEK1-YFPc) were transiently co-expressed in potato protoplasts (Fig. 4A). PMC4765800.p059.s006: Co-expression of YFPn-StWRKY1 and StMEK1-YFPc reconstituted a functional YFP in the nucleus, whereas co-expression with either control vector failed to generate YFP fluorescence, indicating StMEK1 is required for activation of StWRKY1 (Fig. 4B).

events.event_label: pathogen infection upregulates 4-CL, THT and TyDC producing HCAA metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s001: To combat pathogen infection, plants have developed specific metabolic pathways to biosynthesize metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls as mechanical barriers. PMC4765800.p080.s002: Following pathogen inoculation, genes involved in the production of tyramine-derived HCAAs were 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, which were upregulated 2.13-, 2.98-, and 4.04-fold, respectively, in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes.

events.event_label: agroinfiltration induces stunted growth and photobleaching via PDS involvement in carotenoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p066.s003: A mixture of Agrobacterium cultures containing pTRV1 and pTRV2-StWRKY1 or pTRV2-StPDS was infiltrated onto 2-week-old resistant potato seedlings. PMC4765800.p066.s004: Agroinfiltrated plants were healthy and no plant died from this treatment, although stunted growth was observed. PMC4765800.p066.s005: Plants expressing pTRV2-StPDS showed photobleaching after 30 d of agroinfiltration and used as positive controls to identify plants that were successfully silenced (Supplementary Fig. S7B). PMC4765800.p066.s006: The PDS gene was used to demonstrate the effectiveness of VIGS, as this gene participates in the carotenoid metabolic pathway. PMC4765800.p066.s007: StWRKY1-silenced leaves exhibited higher levels of P. infestans susceptibility compared with non-silenced plants.

events.event_label: Silencing StWRKY1 increases disease severity and lesion length, reducing late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s001: To assess the effect of StWRKY1 in late blight resistance, the disease severity and biomass were quantified in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants. PMC4765800.p067.s002: The lesion length was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (26.19mm; AUDPC=80.68), significantly differing from the non-silenced plants (14.90mm; AUDPC=47.60). PMC4765800.p067.s003: Likewise, pathogen biomass was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (fold change=13.06) compared with non-silenced plants at 6 dpi (Fig. 6A, B). PMC4765800.p067.s004: Furthermore, the relative abundances of the transcripts of phenylpropanoid genes (4-CL, THT, TyDC, and HCT) were determined in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants with reference to the susceptible genotype (Fig. 6C–F). PMC4765800.p067.s006: However, no significant reduction was observed in HCT transcripts in silenced plants (fold change=2.22) compared with resistant plants (2.41) and this was probably due to the absence of the W1-box in the HCT promoter (Fig. 6G and Supplementary Table S3). PMC4765800.p067.s007: We then tested the effect of StWRKY1 silencing on the production of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p067.s008: The RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine (fold change=10.13), N-feruloyloctopamine (6.65), feruloylputrescine (3.80), and feruloylagmatine (3.44) were reduced (P<0.05) after silencing (Table 2). PMC4765800.p067.s009: Taken together, these results provide compelling evidence that StWRKY1 is a positive regulator of late blight resistance through the reinforcement of secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 interacts with 4-CL, TyDC, and THT enzymes

events.event_label: HCAAs accumulate in cell wall and contribute to cell wall thickening upon pathogen treatment
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s005: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2). PMC4765800.p051.s006: The expression of RR genes is transcriptionally regulated by the co-ordinated action of transcription factors which bind to cis-acting elements and regulate the expression of RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s007: Previous studies reported that StWRKY1 (GenBank accession number AJ278507.1; chromosome 5) is induced and co-regulated with class I endochitinase during the compatible interaction of potato and P. infestans.

events.event_label: sHSP17.8 regulates StWRKY1 expression

PMC4765800.p055.s002: The relative transcript expression for (A) StWRKY1 and (B) sHSP17.8 was confirmed in the resistant genotype relative to the susceptible genotype following P. infestans and mock-solution inoculation at 48 hpi using qRT-PCR in comparison with reference genes for elongation factor-1α and β-tubulin. PMC4765800.p055.s005: Values are averages of three replicates. PMC4765800.p055.s006: Significant differences in relative expression levels using Student’s t-test: *P<0.05; **P<0.01.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 regulates secondary cell wall strengthening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s001: Quantitative resistance is polygenically controlled and durable, but the underlying molecular and biochemical mechanisms are poorly understood. PMC4765800.p004.s002: Secondary cell wall thickening is a critical process in quantitative resistance, regulated by transcriptional networks. PMC4765800.p004.s003: This paper provides compelling evidence on the functionality of StWRKY1 transcription factor, in a compatible interaction of potato–Phytophthora infestans, to extend our knowledge on the regulation of the metabolic pathway genes leading to strengthening the secondary cell wall. PMC4765800.p004.s004: A metabolomics approach was used to identify resistance-related metabolites belonging to the phenylpropanoid pathway and their biosynthetic genes regulated by StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p004.s009: Silencing of the StWRKY1 gene was associated with signs of reduced late blight resistance by significantly increasing the pathogen biomass and decreasing the abundance of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p004.s010: This study provides convincing evidence on the role of StWRKY1 in the regulation of downstream genes to biosynthesize HCAAs, which are deposited to reinforce secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: PAMPs activate PTI and MAPK signaling
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s001: Plants have evolved intricate strategies to recognize pathogen infection and devise effective defence responses. PMC4765800.p007.s002: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI). PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes. PMC4765800.p007.s006: PAMP also triggers phytohormones, such as jasmonic acid, salicylic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, and gibberellins, to provide broad biochemical resistance and reduce pathogen growth. PMC4765800.p007.s007: These phytohormones fine-tune the pathways involved in the production of secondary metabolites that impart quantitative resistance. PMC4765800.p007.s008: Following recognition of PAMPs, the receptors trigger a phosphorylation cascade through the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signalling pathway, leading to the induction or repression of target genes. PMC4765800.p007.s009: Among the induced target genes are a variety of transcription factors that regulate plant defence responses involving up- or down-regulation of resistance-related (RR) genes by binding to specific DNA sequences in their promoter regions.

events.event_label: HCAAs participate in cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

events.event_label: Secondary cell wall thickening contributes_to Quantitative resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s001: Quantitative resistance is polygenically controlled and durable, but the underlying molecular and biochemical mechanisms are poorly understood. PMC4765800.p004.s002: Secondary cell wall thickening is a critical process in quantitative resistance, regulated by transcriptional networks. PMC4765800.p004.s003: This paper provides compelling evidence on the functionality of StWRKY1 transcription factor, in a compatible interaction of potato–Phytophthora infestans, to extend our knowledge on the regulation of the metabolic pathway genes leading to strengthening the secondary cell wall. PMC4765800.p004.s004: A metabolomics approach was used to identify resistance-related metabolites belonging to the phenylpropanoid pathway and their biosynthetic genes regulated by StWRKY1.

events.event_label: transcription factors affects secondary cell wall biosynthetic genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p006.s006: Cell wall thickening reduces the spread of pathogens in plants beyond the site of infection, reducing lesion expansion. PMC4765800.p006.s007: The biosynthesis of the secondary cell wall is a developmental process that involves the co-ordinated expression of secondary cell wall biosynthetic genes regulated by a cascade of transcription factors. PMC4765800.p006.s008: Understanding such processes is vital for the manipulation of secondary cell wall thickening to resist pathogen development in plants.

events.event_label: transcription factors affects RR
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s006: PAMP also triggers phytohormones, such as jasmonic acid, salicylic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, and gibberellins, to provide broad biochemical resistance and reduce pathogen growth. PMC4765800.p007.s007: These phytohormones fine-tune the pathways involved in the production of secondary metabolites that impart quantitative resistance. PMC4765800.p007.s008: Following recognition of PAMPs, the receptors trigger a phosphorylation cascade through the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signalling pathway, leading to the induction or repression of target genes. PMC4765800.p007.s009: Among the induced target genes are a variety of transcription factors that regulate plant defence responses involving up- or down-regulation of resistance-related (RR) genes by binding to specific DNA sequences in their promoter regions.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 contributes_to quantitative resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s001: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach. PMC4765800.p009.s002: We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype. PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

events.event_label: RR is_detected_in stems
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s001: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach. PMC4765800.p009.s002: We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype. PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 is_detected_in potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases. PMC4765800.p009.s008: WRKYs are implicated in the regulation of plant defence against pathogens, but only a few members of the WRKY superfamily in potato have been isolated or functionally characterized. PMC4765800.p009.s009: StWRKY1 was reported in potato following infection with P. infestans, but no details on metabolic pathway regulation were revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s010: The current study uses a forward genetics approach to discover StWRKY1 from the high fold change in abundance of hydroxycinnamic acid amides (HCAAs) detected based on metabolic profiling, and reverse genetics to validate in depth the transcriptional activation of StWRKY1, possible interactions between StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes, and their link to secondary cell wall strengthening to contain pathogen spread following P. infestans invasion.

events.event_label: inoculation with P. infestans occurs_in stems
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p042.s001: Late blight severity in the potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody was assessed by measuring lesion length over time following inoculation with P. infestans clonal lineage US-8. PMC4765800.p042.s002: The initial symptoms were observed as brown-coloured lesions on the inoculated stems at 3 dpi in both F06025 and Shepody. PMC4765800.p042.s003: By 9 dpi, the stem lesion length was 66.44mm (AUDPC=201.09) in Shepody, differing (P<0.001) from the resistant genotype F06025 with a lesion length of 13.44mm (AUDPC=49.71) (Fig. 1A and Supplementary Fig. S1 at JXB online).

events.event_label: transcription factors changes_expression_of RR genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s005: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2). PMC4765800.p051.s006: The expression of RR genes is transcriptionally regulated by the co-ordinated action of transcription factors which bind to cis-acting elements and regulate the expression of RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s007: Previous studies reported that StWRKY1 (GenBank accession number AJ278507.1; chromosome 5) is induced and co-regulated with class I endochitinase during the compatible interaction of potato and P. infestans.

events.event_label: HCAAs affects secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p064.s001: The HCAAs, including N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylagmatine, and feruloylputrescine, play a critical role in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p064.s002: Accordingly, the physical interaction of StWRKY1 with HCAA biosynthetic gene THT was tested by in vitro EMSA as additional evidence (Supplementary Fig. S6 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p064.s003: For EMSA, the purified recombinant StWRKY1 protein fractions had a molecular weight of 25kDa (Supplementary Fig. S6A) as analysed on 12% SDS-PAGE. PMC4765800.p064.s004: This was in agreement with the theoretical molecular weight of 23kDa (19.86kDa for the native protein plus 3kDa for the His-tag).

events.event_label: HCAAs participates_in phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p073.s001: In the present study, an integrated metabolomics, gene expression, and gene sequencing information approach was used to discover and explore the role of transcription factor StWRKY1, and also to explain the late blight resistance mechanisms in potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody. PMC4765800.p073.s002: These genotypes varied in their resistance levels and also in accumulation of metabolites. PMC4765800.p073.s003: Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs. PMC4765800.p073.s004: These metabolites are well known for their role in cell wall thickening, forming antimicrobial barriers.

events.event_label: infection increases_expression_of StWRKY1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s003: Several studies have focused on model plants such as Arabidopsis and tobacco, but only a few focused on potato WRKYs. PMC4765800.p075.s004: In potato, StWRKY1 is strongly induced following a compatible interaction with P. infestans and treatment with potassium phosphite during infection inhibited pathogen spread. PMC4765800.p075.s005: Overexpression of VvWRKY1 in grapevines provided higher resistance to downy mildew Plasmopara viticola through higher expression of genes involved in the jasmonic acid pathway. PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar.

events.event_label: WRKY affects potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar. PMC4765800.p075.s008: Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix. PMC4765800.p075.s009: These findings depict the involvement of the WRKY transcription factors in disease resistance, as described here in potato against P. infestans.

events.event_label: MAPK localizes_to nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression. PMC4765800.p076.s005: In potato, StMEK1 was functionally characterised during defence reactions, which are also associated with StWRKY1, as was confirmed here by BiFC assay. PMC4765800.p076.s006: StMEK1 and StWRKY1 function together to induce the expression of downstream RR genes involved in plant defence.

events.event_label: pathogen infection affects secondary cell walls
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s001: To combat pathogen infection, plants have developed specific metabolic pathways to biosynthesize metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls as mechanical barriers. PMC4765800.p080.s002: Following pathogen inoculation, genes involved in the production of tyramine-derived HCAAs were 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, which were upregulated 2.13-, 2.98-, and 4.04-fold, respectively, in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes.

relations.subject: PAMPs

evidence.text: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI).

relations.evidence_preview: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s001: Plants have evolved intricate strategies to recognize pathogen infection and devise effective defence responses. PMC4765800.p007.s002: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI). PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes.

This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes.

evidence_context_sentences.text: WRKY domain-containing proteins are one of the largest families of transcriptional regulators in plants.
They have a DNA-binding domain called a W-box (TTGACC/T or WRKYGQK) and are involved in plant immune responses.

Overexpression of WRKY transcription factor increased tolerance to pathogen infection through induction of phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways in grapes and tobacco. In contrast, the overexpression of WRKY reduced resistance mechanisms in rice and Capsicum plants.

Although WRKY proteins might act as crucial nodes in the cross-talk between PTI and ETI, little is known regarding the molecular events occurring during the establishment of resistance or susceptibility to pathogens in plants.

relations.subject: StWRKY1

evidence.text: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach.

PMC4765800.p009.s002: We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype. PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. evidence_context_sentences.text: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach.
We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype.
The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways.

As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

evidence.text: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

relations.evidence_preview: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin. PMC4765800.p009.s005: Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways.

As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants.
Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

WRKYs are implicated in the regulation of plant defence against pathogens, but only a few members of the WRKY superfamily in potato have been isolated or functionally characterized. StWRKY1 was reported in potato following infection with P.

PMC4765800.p009.s010: The current study uses a forward genetics approach to discover StWRKY1 from the high fold change in abundance of hydroxycinnamic acid amides (HCAAs) detected based on metabolic profiling, and reverse genetics to validate in depth the transcriptional activation of StWRKY1, possible interactions between StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes, and their link to secondary cell wall strengthening to contain pathogen spread following P. infestans invasion.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

WRKYs are implicated in the regulation of plant defence against pathogens, but only a few members of the WRKY superfamily in potato have been isolated or functionally characterized. StWRKY1 was reported in potato following infection with P.

infestans, but no details on metabolic pathway regulation were revealed. The current study uses a forward genetics approach to discover StWRKY1 from the high fold change in abundance of hydroxycinnamic acid amides (HCAAs) detected based on metabolic profiling, and reverse genetics to validate in depth the transcriptional activation of StWRKY1, possible interactions between StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes, and their link to secondary cell wall strengthening to contain pathogen spread following P.
infestans invasion.

The current study uses a forward genetics approach to discover StWRKY1 from the high fold change in abundance of hydroxycinnamic acid amides (HCAAs) detected based on metabolic profiling, and reverse genetics to validate in depth the transcriptional activation of StWRKY1, possible interactions between StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes, and their link to secondary cell wall strengthening to contain pathogen spread following P. infestans invasion.

relations.subject: N-feruloyltyramine

evidence.text: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

relations.subject: pathogen infection

evidence.text: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)].

relations.evidence_preview: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)].
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression. PMC4765800.p059.s005: To substantiate the interaction between StWRKY1 and StMEK1 in potato cells, a BiFC assay was performed in which YFPn fused to StWRKY1 (YFPn-StWRKY1) and YFPc fused to StMEK1 (StMEK1-YFPc) were transiently co-expressed in potato protoplasts (Fig. 4A). PMC4765800.p059.s006: Co-expression of YFPn-StWRKY1 and StMEK1-YFPc reconstituted a functional YFP in the nucleus, whereas co-expression with either control vector failed to generate YFP fluorescence, indicating StMEK1 is required for activation of StWRKY1 (Fig. 4B).

evidence.text: We found that StWRKY1 bound to the promoter region of RR genes, and drastically activated LUC reporter expression in 4-CL (45.55) and THT (47.00) promoters compared with vector alone (4.16) (Fig. 5B), suggesting the transcriptional regulation of HCAA biosynthesis.

evidence.text: Agroinfiltrated plants were healthy and no plant died from this treatment, although stunted growth was observed.

relations.evidence_preview: Agroinfiltrated plants were healthy and no plant died from this treatment, although stunted growth was observed.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p066.s003: A mixture of Agrobacterium cultures containing pTRV1 and pTRV2-StWRKY1 or pTRV2-StPDS was infiltrated onto 2-week-old resistant potato seedlings. PMC4765800.p066.s004: Agroinfiltrated plants were healthy and no plant died from this treatment, although stunted growth was observed. PMC4765800.p066.s005: Plants expressing pTRV2-StPDS showed photobleaching after 30 d of agroinfiltration and used as positive controls to identify plants that were successfully silenced (Supplementary Fig. S7B). PMC4765800.p066.s006: The PDS gene was used to demonstrate the effectiveness of VIGS, as this gene participates in the carotenoid metabolic pathway. PMC4765800.p066.s007: StWRKY1-silenced leaves exhibited higher levels of P. infestans susceptibility compared with non-silenced plants.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A mixture of Agrobacterium cultures containing pTRV1 and pTRV2-StWRKY1 or pTRV2-StPDS was infiltrated onto 2-week-old resistant potato seedlings.

The PDS gene was used to demonstrate the effectiveness of VIGS, as this gene participates in the carotenoid metabolic pathway.
StWRKY1-silenced leaves exhibited higher levels of P.
infestans susceptibility compared with non-silenced plants.

relations.subject: HCAAs

evidence.text: Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs.

relations.evidence_preview: Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p073.s001: In the present study, an integrated metabolomics, gene expression, and gene sequencing information approach was used to discover and explore the role of transcription factor StWRKY1, and also to explain the late blight resistance mechanisms in potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody. PMC4765800.p073.s002: These genotypes varied in their resistance levels and also in accumulation of metabolites. PMC4765800.p073.s003: Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs. PMC4765800.p073.s004: These metabolites are well known for their role in cell wall thickening, forming antimicrobial barriers.

evidence_context_sentences.text: In the present study, an integrated metabolomics, gene expression, and gene sequencing information approach was used to discover and explore the role of transcription factor StWRKY1, and also to explain the late blight resistance mechanisms in potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody.
These genotypes varied in their resistance levels and also in accumulation of metabolites.
Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs.
These metabolites are well known for their role in cell wall thickening, forming antimicrobial barriers.

evidence_context_sentences.text: In potato, StWRKY1 is strongly induced following a compatible interaction with P.
infestans and treatment with potassium phosphite during infection inhibited pathogen spread.
Overexpression of VvWRKY1 in grapevines provided higher resistance to downy mildew Plasmopara viticola through higher expression of genes involved in the jasmonic acid pathway.

Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways.

evidence.text: The transcript abundance of StWRKY1 and its regulating RRI genes, 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, was reduced (P<0.05) in silenced plants (fold change=1.16, 0.40, 0.44, and 0.71, respectively) compared with resistant plants (5.52, 2.13, 2.98, and 4.04, respectively).

relations.evidence_preview: The transcript abundance of StWRKY1 and its regulating RRI genes, 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, was reduced (P<0.05) in silenced plants (fold change=1.16, 0.40, 0.44, and 0.71, respectively) compared with resistant plants (5.52, 2.13, 2.98, and 4.04, respectively).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p082.s001: To further assess the effect of StWRKY1 on the resistance mechanisms, StWRKY1 was silenced in a resistant genotype, and silenced and non-silenced resistant plants were compared based on relative gene expression of HCAA biosynthetic genes, accumulation of their downstream biosynthesized RRI metabolites, amount of fungal biomass, and disease severity. PMC4765800.p082.s002: The transcript abundance of StWRKY1 and its regulating RRI genes, 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, was reduced (P<0.05) in silenced plants (fold change=1.16, 0.40, 0.44, and 0.71, respectively) compared with resistant plants (5.52, 2.13, 2.98, and 4.04, respectively). PMC4765800.p082.s003: However, no significant reduction was observed for HCT transcripts, probably due to the absence of the W1-box. PMC4765800.p082.s004: The abundance of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine was reduced 2-fold after silencing, resulting in a higher (P<0.001) biomass in silenced plants (fold change=13.06).

entities.selected_description: Any of the naturally occurring, basic nitrogen compounds (mostly heterocyclic) occurring mostly in the plant kingdom, but also found in bacteria, fungi, and animals. By extension, certain neutral compounds biogenetically related to basic alkaloids are also classed as alkaloids. Amino acids, peptides, proteins, nucleotides, nucleic acids, amino sugars and antibiotics are not normally regarded as alkaloids. Compounds in which the nitrogen is exocyclic (dopamine, mescaline, serotonin, etc.) are usually classed as amines rather than alkaloids.

Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix.
Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix.

Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways.

entities.selected_description: The rigid or semi-rigid envelope lying outside the cell membrane of plant, fungal, most prokaryotic cells and some protozoan parasites, maintaining their shape and protecting them from osmotic lysis. In plants it is made of cellulose and, often, lignin; in fungi it is composed largely of polysaccharides; in bacteria it is composed of peptidoglycan; in protozoan parasites such as Giardia species, it's made of carbohydrates and proteins.

entities.evidence_preview: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach.

Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening.

We found that StWRKY1 bound to the promoter region of RR genes, and drastically activated LUC reporter expression in 4-CL (45.55) and THT (47.00) promoters compared with vector alone (4.16) (Fig. 5B), suggesting the transcriptional regulation of HCAA biosynthesis.

Following recognition of PAMPs, the receptors trigger a phosphorylation cascade through the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signalling pathway, leading to the induction or repression of target genes.

entities.selected_description: An intracellular protein kinase cascade containing at least a MAP kinase (MAPK). It starts with the activation of a MAP3K, and the consecutive activation of a MPK2K and a MAPK. The cascade can also contain an additional tier: the upstream MAP4K. The kinases in each tier phosphorylate and activate the kinase in the downstream tier to transmit a signal within a cell.

selected_concepts.label: MAPK cascade
selected_concepts.description: An intracellular protein kinase cascade containing at least a MAP kinase (MAPK). It starts with the activation of a MAP3K, and the consecutive activation of a MPK2K and a MAPK. The cascade can also contain an additional tier: the upstream MAP4K. The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2).
Transcriptional regulation of phenylpropanoid pathway RRI genes by StWRKY1

The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.
